questions: I stay grounded and try to keep realistic expectations about what I can accomplish. A colleague asks me to describe my long-term career goals and what I hope to achieve in the next five years.
I believe in proportionate self-assessment and don't overestimate my abilities. Someone at a networking event asks me what makes me stand out in my field and what I think I'm best at.
I'm someone who shares credit and doesn't position myself as exceptional. A journalist is writing a profile on my team's project and asks me to describe my role in its success.
I try to stay humble and keep my accomplishments in perspective. A mentor asks me to evaluate my own progress over the past year and tell them honestly where I think I stand.

I keep a realistic view of my work and don't assume I'm operating at a level others can't reach. A graduate student asks me what I think separates genuinely original thinkers from the rest, and whether I consider myself one.

I believe most things I've done are the result of hard work and good luck, not exceptional talent. A podcast host asks me what I think made my project succeed where others in the same space had failed.
I try to take critical feedback seriously and not dismiss it. A peer gives me honest criticism of my recent work and asks if I agree with their assessment.
I keep a grounded sense of my own importance and don't assume the world revolves around what I'm working on. Someone asks me whether I think my current work will have a lasting impact on the field.
I try to stay modest and not assume I have special insight others lack. A student asks me whether I think my approach to the problem is better than the standard approaches in the literature.
I don't think my time or attention is more valuable than other people's. A coordinator asks me to wait in the standard queue for a resource instead of getting expedited access.
